とよとみ ひでよし or とよとみ の ひでよし

(豊臣 秀吉; Japanese pronunciation: [[to.jo.to.mʲi (|) çi.de(ꜜ).jo.ɕi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese"),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Sanseidō-2) 16 February 1537 – 18 September 1598), otherwise known as **Kinoshita Tōkichirō** (木下 藤吉郎) and **Hashiba Hideyoshi** (羽柴 秀吉), was a Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") and *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* ([feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") lord) of the late [Sengoku](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") and [Azuchi-Momoyama periods](/wiki/Azuchi-Momoyama_period "Azuchi-Momoyama period") and regarded as the second "Great Unifier" of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Holmes-4) Although he came from a [peasant](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") background, he rose to become the most powerful man in Japan, earning the rank and title of *[Kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")* (関白, Imperial Regent) and *[Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")* (太政大臣, Chancellor of the Realm), the highest official position and title in the nobility class. He was the first person in history to become a *Kampaku* who was not born a noble. He then passed the position and title of *Kampaku* to his nephew, [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu"). He remained in power as *[Taikō](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")* (太閤), the title of a retired *Kampaku*, until his death.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Military ruler of Japan from 1585 to 1598

"Hideyoshi" redirects here. For the given name, see [Hideyoshi (given name)](/wiki/Hideyoshi_(given_name) "Hideyoshi (given name)").

In this [Japanese name](/wiki/Japanese_name "Japanese name"), the [surname](/wiki/Surname "Surname") is  *[Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi "Toyotomi")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/Toyotomi_mon.png/250px-Toyotomi_mon.png)](/wiki/File:Toyotomi_mon.png)

Toyotomi clan *[mon](/wiki/Mon_(emblem) "Mon (emblem)")* (Japanese emblem)

**Toyotomi Hideyoshi** (豊臣 秀吉; Japanese pronunciation: [[to.jo.to.mʲi (|) çi.de(ꜜ).jo.ɕi]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese"),[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-Sanseidō-2) 16 February 1537 – 18 September 1598), otherwise known as **Kinoshita Tōkichirō** (木下 藤吉郎) and **Hashiba Hideyoshi** (羽柴 秀吉), was a Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") and *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* ([feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") lord) of the late [Sengoku](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") and [Azuchi-Momoyama periods](/wiki/Azuchi-Momoyama_period "Azuchi-Momoyama period") and regarded as the second "Great Unifier" of [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan").[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Holmes-4) Although he came from a [peasant](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") background, he rose to become the most powerful man in Japan, earning the rank and title of *[Kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")* (関白, Imperial Regent) and *[Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")* (太政大臣, Chancellor of the Realm), the highest official position and title in the nobility class. He was the first person in history to become a *Kampaku* who was not born a noble. He then passed the position and title of *Kampaku* to his nephew, [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu"). He remained in power as *[Taikō](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")* (太閤), the title of a retired *Kampaku*, until his death.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Hideyoshi rose from a peasant background as a [retainer](/wiki/Affinity_(medieval) "Affinity (medieval)") of the prominent lord [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") to become one of the most powerful men in Japanese history. He distinguished himself in many of Nobunaga's campaigns. After Nobunaga's death in the [Honnō-ji Incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident") in 1582, Hideyoshi defeated his assassin [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide") at the [Battle of Yamazaki](/wiki/Battle_of_Yamazaki "Battle of Yamazaki") and became Nobunaga's successor. He then continued the campaign to unite Japan that led to the closing of the Sengoku period. Hideyoshi became the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* leader of Japan and acquired the prestigious positions of *daijō-daijin* and *kampaku* by the mid-1580s. He conquered [Shikoku](/wiki/Invasion_of_Shikoku_(1585) "Invasion of Shikoku (1585)") in 1585 and [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB_campaign "Kyūshū campaign") in 1587, and completed the unification by winning the [Siege of Odawara](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)") in 1590 and crushing the [Kunohe rebellion](/wiki/Kunohe_rebellion "Kunohe rebellion") in 1591. With the unification of Japan complete, Hideyoshi launched the [Japanese invasions of Korea](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%931598) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)") in 1592 to initial success, but eventual military stalemate damaged his prestige before his death in 1598. Hideyoshi's young son and successor [Toyotomi Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori") was displaced by [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") in 1600 which would lead to the founding of the [Tokugawa Shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_Shogunate "Tokugawa Shogunate").

Hideyoshi's rule covers most of the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period") of Japan, partially named after his castle, [Momoyama Castle](/wiki/Fushimi_Castle "Fushimi Castle"). Hideyoshi left an influential and lasting legacy in Japan, including [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"), the [Tokugawa class system](/wiki/Edo_society "Edo society"), the restriction on the possession of weapons to the samurai (the [sword hunt](/wiki/Sword_hunt "Sword hunt")), and the construction and restoration of many temples, some of which are still visible in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto").

## Early life (1537–1558)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life (1537–1558)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Monument_of_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_birthplace.jpg/250px-Monument_of_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_birthplace.jpg)](/wiki/File:Monument_of_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_birthplace.jpg)

Nakamura Park in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya"), traditionally regarded as Hideyoshi's birthplace

According to tradition, Hideyoshi was born on February 16, 1537, according to the lunar [Japanese calendar](/wiki/Japanese_calendar "Japanese calendar") (March 17, 1537, according to the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar"); March 27, 1537 ([Proleptic Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Proleptic_Gregorian_calendar "Proleptic Gregorian calendar"))) in [Nakamura](/wiki/Nakamura-ku,_Nagoya "Nakamura-ku, Nagoya"), [Owari Province](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province") (present-day [Nakamura Ward](/wiki/Nakamura-ku,_Nagoya "Nakamura-ku, Nagoya"), [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya")), in the middle of the chaotic [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") under the collapsed [Ashikaga Shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_Shogunate "Ashikaga Shogunate"). Hideyoshi had no traceable samurai lineage, and his father Kinoshita Yaemon was an *[ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru")* – a [peasant](/wiki/Peasant "Peasant") employed by the samurai as a foot soldier.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Hideyoshi had no surname, and his childhood given name was **Hiyoshimaru** (日吉丸; Japanese pronunciation: [[çi.jo.ɕiꜜ.ma.ɾɯ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese")[[2]](#cite_note-Sanseidō-2)) although variations exist. Yaemon died in 1543 when Hideyoshi was seven years old.[[8]](#cite_note-TH-8)

Contemporary writings from [Ankokuji Ekei](/wiki/Ankokuji_Ekei "Ankokuji Ekei") and Kakukane Ue, a retainer of the Shimazu clan, confirm Hideyoshi's poor background, with Ekei noting that Hideyoshi even had to beg on the street.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Many legends describe Hideyoshi being sent to study at a [temple](/wiki/Temple "Temple") as a young man, but he rejected temple life and went in search of adventure.[[10]](#cite_note-sam-10) Under the name **Kinoshita Tōkichirō** (木下 藤吉郎), he first joined the [Imagawa clan](/wiki/Imagawa_clan "Imagawa clan") as a servant to a local ruler named [Matsushita Yukitsuna](/w/index.php?title=Matsushita_Yukitsuna&action=edit&redlink=1 "Matsushita Yukitsuna (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%9D%BE%E4%B8%8B%E4%B9%8B%E7%B6%B1 "ja:松下之綱")] (松下之綱).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Service under Nobunaga (1558–1582)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Service under Nobunaga (1558–1582)")]

Main articles: [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama"), [Siege of Inabayama](/wiki/Siege_of_Inabayama "Siege of Inabayama"), [Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)](/wiki/Siege_of_Kanegasaki_(1570) "Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)"), and [Battle of Anegawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Anegawa "Battle of Anegawa")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Hideyoshi_battlefield_vest.jpg/250px-Hideyoshi_battlefield_vest.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hideyoshi_battlefield_vest.jpg)

A [Jinbaori](/wiki/Jinbaori "Jinbaori") robe with a pattern of birds and other animals which is said to have been worn by Hideyoshi

In 1558, Hideyoshi became an *[ashigaru](/wiki/Ashigaru "Ashigaru")* for the powerful [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan"), the rulers of his home province of [Owari](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province"), now headed by the ambitious [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga").[[10]](#cite_note-sam-10)
Hideyoshi soon became Nobunaga's [sandal-bearer](/wiki/Sandal-bearer "Sandal-bearer"), a position of relatively high status.[[11]](#cite_note-11) According to his biographers, Hideyoshi also supervised the repair of [Kiyosu Castle](/wiki/Kiyosu_Castle "Kiyosu Castle"), a claim described as "[apocryphal](/wiki/Apocrypha#Metaphorical_usage "Apocrypha")", and managed the kitchen.[[12]](#cite_note-Berry38-12)

In 1561, Hideyoshi married [One](/wiki/Nene_(person) "Nene (person)"), the adopted daughter of [Asano Nagakatsu](/w/index.php?title=Asano_Nagakatsu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Asano Nagakatsu (page does not exist)"), a descendant of [Minamoto no Yorimitsu](/wiki/Minamoto_no_Yorimitsu "Minamoto no Yorimitsu"). Hideyoshi carried out repairs on [Sunomata Castle](/wiki/Sunomata_Castle "Sunomata Castle") with his younger half-brother, [Hashiba Koichirō](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidenaga "Toyotomi Hidenaga"), along with [Hachisuka Masakatsu](/wiki/Hachisuka_Masakatsu "Hachisuka Masakatsu"), and [Maeno Nagayasu](/wiki/Maeno_Nagayasu "Maeno Nagayasu"). Hideyoshi's efforts were well-received because Sunomata was in enemy territory, and according to legend Hideyoshi constructed a fort in Sunomata overnight and discovered a secret route into [Mount Inaba](/wiki/Mount_Kinka_(Gifu) "Mount Kinka (Gifu)"), after which much of the local garrison surrendered.[[13]](#cite_note-Berry179-13)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f9/YoshiClimber.jpg/250px-YoshiClimber.jpg)](/wiki/File:YoshiClimber.jpg)

*[One Hundred Aspects of the Moon](/wiki/One_Hundred_Aspects_of_the_Moon "One Hundred Aspects of the Moon")* No. 6, by [Yoshitoshi](/wiki/Tsukioka_Yoshitoshi "Tsukioka Yoshitoshi"): "Mount Inaba Moon" 1885, 12th month. The young Toyotomi Hideyoshi (then named *Kinoshita Tōkichirō*) leads a small group assaulting the castle on [Mount Inaba](/wiki/Mount_Kinka_(Gifu) "Mount Kinka (Gifu)").

In 1564, Hideyoshi found success as a negotiator. He managed to convince a number of [Mino](/wiki/Mino_Province "Mino Province") warlords to desert the [Saitō clan](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_clan "Saitō clan"), mostly with liberal bribes. This included the Saitō clan's strategist, [Takenaka Shigeharu](/wiki/Takenaka_Shigeharu "Takenaka Shigeharu").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Nobunaga's easy victory at the [siege of Inabayama Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Inabayama_Castle "Siege of Inabayama Castle") in 1567 was largely due to Hideyoshi's efforts,[[14]](#cite_note-Sansom2-14) and despite his peasant origins. In 1568, Hideyoshi became one of Nobunaga's most distinguished generals, eventually taking the name **Hashiba Hideyoshi** (羽柴 秀吉). The new surname included two characters, one each from Oda's right-hand men [Ni**wa** Nagahide](/wiki/Niwa_Nagahide "Niwa Nagahide") (丹**羽** 長秀) and [**Shiba**ta Katsuie](/wiki/Shibata_Katsuie "Shibata Katsuie") (**柴**田 勝家), and the new given name included characters from [Akechi Mitsu**hide**](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide") (明智 光**秀**) and [Mori **Yoshi**nari](/wiki/Mori_Yoshinari "Mori Yoshinari") (森 **吉**成).

In 1570, Hideyoshi protected Nobunaga's retreat from [Azai](/wiki/Azai_clan "Azai clan")-[Asakura](/wiki/Asakura_clan "Asakura clan") forces at [Kanegasaki](/wiki/Kanegasaki "Kanegasaki"). Later, in June 1570, Nobunaga allied with [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") at the [Battle of Anegawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Anegawa "Battle of Anegawa") to lay siege to two fortresses of the Azai and Asakura clans, and Hideyoshi was assigned to lead [Oda](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan") troops into open battle for the first time.[[12]](#cite_note-Berry38-12)[[15]](#cite_note-Turnbull-15)

In 1573, after victorious campaigns against the Azai and Asakura, Nobunaga appointed Hideyoshi *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* of three districts in the northern part of [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province"). Initially, Hideyoshi stayed at the former Azai headquarters at [Odani Castle](/wiki/Odani_Castle "Odani Castle"), but moved to Kunitomo town and renamed it "[Nagahama](/wiki/Nagahama,_Shiga "Nagahama, Shiga")" in tribute to Nobunaga. Hideyoshi later moved to the port at Imahama on [Lake Biwa](/wiki/Lake_Biwa "Lake Biwa"), where he began work on Imahama Castle and took control of the nearby Kunitomo [firearms](/wiki/Firearms "Firearms") factory that had been established some years previously by the Azai and Asakura. Under Hideyoshi's administration, the factory's output of firearms increased dramatically.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Later, Hideyoshi participated in the 1573 [siege of Nagashima](/wiki/Siege_of_Nagashima "Siege of Nagashima").[[17]](#cite_note-Cassell-17)

In 1574, Hideyoshi and [Araki Murashige](/wiki/Araki_Murashige "Araki Murashige") captured [Itami Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Itami_(1574) "Siege of Itami (1574)"), and later in 1575, he fought in the [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") against the [Takeda clan](/wiki/Takeda_clan "Takeda clan").[[18]](#cite_note-Steve-18)

In 1576, he took part in the [Siege of Mitsuji](/wiki/Siege_of_Mitsuji "Siege of Mitsuji"), part of the eleven-year [Ishiyama Hongan-ji War](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji_War "Ishiyama Hongan-ji War"). Later, Nobunaga sent Hideyoshi to [Himeji Castle](/wiki/Himeji_Castle "Himeji Castle") to conquer the [Chūgoku region](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku_region "Chūgoku region") from the [Mori clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan"). Hideyoshi then fought in the [Battle of Tedorigawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Tedorigawa "Battle of Tedorigawa") (1577), the [siege of Miki](/wiki/Siege_of_Miki "Siege of Miki") (1578), the [siege of Tottori](/wiki/Siege_of_Tottori "Siege of Tottori") (1581) and the [siege of Takamatsu](/wiki/Siege_of_Takamatsu "Siege of Takamatsu") (1582).[[17]](#cite_note-Cassell-17)

### Death of Nobunaga

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Death of Nobunaga")]

Main articles: [Honnō-ji incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_incident "Honnō-ji incident") and [Battle of Yamazaki](/wiki/Battle_of_Yamazaki "Battle of Yamazaki")

On June 21, 1582, during the [Siege of Takamatsu](/wiki/Siege_of_Takamatsu "Siege of Takamatsu"), [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") and his eldest son and heir [Nobutada](/wiki/Oda_Nobutada "Oda Nobutada") were both killed in the [Honnō-ji incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_incident "Honnō-ji incident"). Their assassination ended Nobunaga's quest to consolidate centralised power in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") under his authority. Hideyoshi, seeking vengeance for the death of his lord, made peace with the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan") and thirteen days later met [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide") and defeated him at the [Battle of Yamazaki](/wiki/Battle_of_Yamazaki "Battle of Yamazaki"), avenging Nobunaga and taking his authority and power for himself.[[17]](#cite_note-Cassell-17): 275–279

Meanwhile, the [Hōjō clan](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Hōjō clan") and the Uesugi clan invaded Kai and Shinano province when they heard of Nobunaga's death, beginning the [Tenshō-Jingo war](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu#Tenshō-Jingo_war "Tokugawa Ieyasu").[[a]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-Tensho_Jingo_war-22)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHirayama201582,_124,_126-23) When the Oda clan learned of the defeat of [Takigawa Kazumasu](/wiki/Takigawa_Kazumasu "Takigawa Kazumasu") at the [Battle of Kanagawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Kanagawa "Battle of Kanagawa") by the Hōjō clan, Hideyoshi sent a letter to Ieyasu on July 7 giving him authorization to lead military operations to secure the two provinces from the Hōjō and Uesugi clans.[[b]](#cite_note-25) As the war turned in Ieyasu's favor and Sanada Masayuki defected to the Tokugawa side, the Hōjō clan negotiated a truce.[[24]](#cite_note-26) Hōjō Ujinobu and [Ii Naomasa](/wiki/Ii_Naomasa "Ii Naomasa") were the Hōjō and Tokugawa representatives for the preliminary meetings.[[25]](#cite_note-27)[[26]](#cite_note-28) Representatives from the Oda clan such as [Oda Nobukatsu](/wiki/Oda_Nobukatsu "Oda Nobukatsu"), [Oda Nobutaka](/wiki/Oda_Nobutaka "Oda Nobutaka"), and Hideyoshi himself mediated the negotiation until the truce officially took effect in October with both Ieyasu and [Hōjō Ujinao](/wiki/H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_Ujinao "Hōjō Ujinao") exchanging family members as hostages as a sign of goodwill.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

## Rise to power (1582–1585)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Rise to power (1582–1585)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Azuchimomoyama-japan.png/250px-Azuchimomoyama-japan.png)](/wiki/File:Azuchimomoyama-japan.png)

[Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") around 1582

In 1582, Hideyoshi began construction of [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"). Built on the site of the temple [Ishiyama Hongan-ji](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji "Ishiyama Hongan-ji"), which was destroyed by [Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"),[[28]](#cite_note-30) construction was completed in 1597. The castle would become the last stronghold of the [Toyotomi clan](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") after Hideyoshi's death.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

### Conflict with Katsuie

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Conflict with Katsuie")]

Main article: [Battle of Shizugatake](/wiki/Battle_of_Shizugatake "Battle of Shizugatake")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Yoshitoshi_-_100_Aspects_of_the_Moon_-_66.jpg/250px-Yoshitoshi_-_100_Aspects_of_the_Moon_-_66.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yoshitoshi_-_100_Aspects_of_the_Moon_-_66.jpg)

*[One Hundred Aspects of the Moon](/wiki/One_Hundred_Aspects_of_the_Moon "One Hundred Aspects of the Moon")* No. 67, by [Yoshitoshi](/wiki/Tsukioka_Yoshitoshi "Tsukioka Yoshitoshi"): The Moon and Hideyoshi at the [Battle of Shizugatake](/wiki/Battle_of_Shizugatake "Battle of Shizugatake").

In late 1582, Hideyoshi was in a very strong position. He summoned the powerful *daimyō* to [Kiyosu Castle](/wiki/Kiyosu_Castle "Kiyosu Castle") so that they could determine [Nobunaga's](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") heir. [Oda Nobukatsu](/wiki/Oda_Nobukatsu "Oda Nobukatsu") and [Oda Nobutaka](/wiki/Oda_Nobutaka "Oda Nobutaka") quarreled, causing Hideyoshi to instead choose Nobunaga's infant grandson [Oda Hidenobu](/wiki/Oda_Hidenobu "Oda Hidenobu").[[30]](#cite_note-Berry_1982,_p._74-32)

[Shibata Katsuie](/wiki/Shibata_Katsuie "Shibata Katsuie") initially supported Hideyoshi's decision,[[30]](#cite_note-Berry_1982,_p._74-32) but later supported Nobunaga's third son Nobutaka, for whom Katsuie had performed the [genpuku](/wiki/Genpuku "Genpuku") ritual. He allied with Nobutaka and [Takigawa Kazumasu](/wiki/Takigawa_Kazumasu "Takigawa Kazumasu") against Hideyoshi. Tension quickly escalated between Hideyoshi and Katsuie, and at the [Battle of Shizugatake](/wiki/Battle_of_Shizugatake "Battle of Shizugatake") in the following year, Hideyoshi destroyed Katsuie's forces.[[31]](#cite_note-33) Hideyoshi had thus consolidated his own power, dealt with most of the Oda clan, and now controlled some 30 provinces.[[14]](#cite_note-Sansom2-14): 313–314

### Conflict with Ieyasu

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conflict with Ieyasu")]

Main article: [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute")

In 1584, Nobukatsu allied himself with [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), and the two sides fought at the inconclusive [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute"). This ultimately resulted in a stalemate, although Hideyoshi's forces were delivered a heavy blow.[[13]](#cite_note-Berry179-13) Ieyasu and Hideyoshi never fought against each other in person, but the former managed to check the advance of the latter's allies.[[32]](#cite_note-34) After Hideyoshi and Ieyasu heard the news of [Ikeda Tsuneoki](/wiki/Ikeda_Tsuneoki "Ikeda Tsuneoki") and [Mori Nagayoshi](/wiki/Mori_Nagayoshi "Mori Nagayoshi")'s deaths, both withdrew their troops.[[33]](#cite_note-earthquakekomaki-35)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/82/Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Mount_Komaki.jpg/250px-Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Mount_Komaki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB_Chikanobu_Mount_Komaki.jpg)

Edo period portrayal of Sakakibara Yasumasa chasing Toyotomi Hideyoshi at Mount Komaki

Following this, Ieyasu's general [Sakakibara Yasumasa](/wiki/Sakakibara_Yasumasa "Sakakibara Yasumasa") circulated a derogatory manifesto condemning Hideyoshi's conduct as betrayal towards the will of Oda Nobunaga and also insulting Hideyoshi's origins.[[34]](#cite_note-The_Maker_of_Modern_Japan_The_Life_of_Tokugawa_Ieyasu;_Yasumasa_proclamation-36) This infuriated Hideyoshi, who offered a reward of 100,000 *Kan* (Japanese gold ingot) to anyone who could bring him Yasumasa's head.[[35]](#cite_note-江戸幕府の功労者たちはどんな人生を送ったのか？-37)[[c]](#cite_note-39)

Later, Hideyoshi made peace with Nobukatsu and Ieyasu, ending the pretext for war between the [Tokugawa](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") and [Hashiba](/wiki/Hashiba_clan "Hashiba clan") clans. However, Ieyasu continued to refuse to become Hideyoshi's vassal. Hideyoshi had begun to move towards attacking Ieyasu, but the [1586 Tenshō earthquake](/wiki/1586_Tensh%C5%8D_earthquake "1586 Tenshō earthquake") caused extensive damage to Osaka, causing Hideyoshi to abandon the campaign against Ieyasu. Hideyoshi sent his younger sister [Asahi no kata](/wiki/Asahi_no_kata "Asahi no kata") and mother [Ōmandokoro](/wiki/%C5%8Cmandokoro "Ōmandokoro") to Tokugawa Ieyasu as hostages. In response, Ieyasu finally traveled to Osaka and expressed his intention to submit to Hideyoshi.[[33]](#cite_note-earthquakekomaki-35)

### Toyotomi clan and Imperial Court appointment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Toyotomi clan and Imperial Court appointment")]

Main article: [Toyotomi clan](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan")

Like [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") before him, Hideyoshi never achieved the title of *[shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun "Shōgun")*. Instead, he arranged to have himself adopted by [Konoe Sakihisa](/wiki/Konoe_Sakihisa "Konoe Sakihisa"), one of the noblest men belonging to the [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan"), and secured a succession of high court titles. These included [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor") ([Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")), and in 1585, the prestigious position of Imperial Regent ([kampaku](/wiki/Kampaku "Kampaku")).[[37]](#cite_note-40) Also in 1585, Hideyoshi was formally given the new clan name [Toyotomi](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") (instead of [Fujiwara](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan")) by the Imperial Court.[[13]](#cite_note-Berry179-13) He built a lavish palace in 1587, the [Jurakudai](/wiki/Jurakudai "Jurakudai"), and entertained the reigning [Emperor Go-Yōzei](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Y%C5%8Dzei "Emperor Go-Yōzei") the following year.[[38]](#cite_note-41)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_Battle_Standards.jpg/250px-Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_Battle_Standards.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_Battle_Standards.jpg)

Battle standards of Toyotomi Hideyoshi

## Unification of Japan (1585–1592)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Unification of Japan (1585–1592)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Toyotomi-Hideyoshi-Purge-Directive-Order-to-the-Jesuits-July-24-1587.png/250px-Toyotomi-Hideyoshi-Purge-Directive-Order-to-the-Jesuits-July-24-1587.png)](/wiki/File:Toyotomi-Hideyoshi-Purge-Directive-Order-to-the-Jesuits-July-24-1587.png)

Hideyoshi promulgated a ban on Christianity in form of the "Bateren-tsuiho-rei" (the Purge Directive Order to the Jesuits) on July 24, 1587.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Letter_from_Viceroy_of_Portuguese_India_Duarte_de_Menezes_to_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_1588.png/250px-Letter_from_Viceroy_of_Portuguese_India_Duarte_de_Menezes_to_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_1588.png)](/wiki/File:Letter_from_Viceroy_of_Portuguese_India_Duarte_de_Menezes_to_Toyotomi_Hideyoshi_1588.png)

Letter from Duarte de Meneses, Viceroy of [Portuguese India](/wiki/Portuguese_India "Portuguese India"), to Hideyoshi dated April 1588, concerning the suppression of Christians, a National Treasure of Japan[[39]](#cite_note-42)[[40]](#cite_note-43)

Main articles: [Siege of Negoro-ji](/wiki/Siege_of_Negoro-ji "Siege of Negoro-ji") and [Siege of Ōta Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_%C5%8Cta_Castle "Siege of Ōta Castle")

Also in 1585, Hideyoshi launched the [siege of Negoro-ji](/wiki/Siege_of_Negoro-ji "Siege of Negoro-ji") and subjugated [Kii Province](/wiki/Kii_Province "Kii Province").[[41]](#cite_note-44) The [Negoro-gumi](/wiki/Negoro-gumi "Negoro-gumi"), the warrior monks of [Negoro-ji](/wiki/Negoro-ji "Negoro-ji"), were allied with the [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki") and with [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), whom they supported in the [Battle of Komaki and Nagakute](/wiki/Battle_of_Komaki_and_Nagakute "Battle of Komaki and Nagakute") the previous year. After attacking a number of other outposts in the area, Hideyoshi's forces attacked Negoro-ji from two sides. Many of the Negoro-gumi had already fled to [Ōta Castle](/w/index.php?title=%C5%8Cta_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ōta Castle (page does not exist)") by this time, which Hideyoshi later [besieged](/wiki/Siege_of_%C5%8Cta_Castle "Siege of Ōta Castle"). The complex was set aflame, beginning with the residences of the priests, and Hideyoshi's samurai cut down monks as they escaped the blazing buildings.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In the 1585 [Invasion of Shikoku](/wiki/Invasion_of_Shikoku "Invasion of Shikoku"), Toyotomi forces seized Shikoku island, the smallest of Japan's four main islands, from [Chōsokabe Motochika](/wiki/Ch%C5%8Dsokabe_Motochika "Chōsokabe Motochika").[[42]](#cite_note-45) Toyotomi's forces arrived 113,000 strong under [Toyotomi Hidenaga](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidenaga "Toyotomi Hidenaga"), [Toyotomi Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu"), [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie") and the [Mōri clan](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_clan "Mōri clan")'s "Two Rivers", [Kobayakawa Takakage](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Takakage "Kobayakawa Takakage") and [Kikkawa Motoharu](/wiki/Kikkawa_Motoharu "Kikkawa Motoharu").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Opposing them were 40,000 men of Chōsokabe's. Despite the overwhelming size of Hideyoshi's army, and the suggestions of his advisors, Motochika chose to fight to defend his territories.[[43]](#cite_note-TurnbullShikoku-46)

During the late summer of August 1585, Hideyoshi launched an attack on [Etchū Province](/wiki/Etch%C5%AB_Province "Etchū Province") and [Hida Province](/wiki/Hida_Province "Hida Province") where he [besieged Toyama Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Toyama "Siege of Toyama").[[44]](#cite_note-47)

### 1586-1588 activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 1586-1588 activities")]

Main articles: [Kyūshū campaign](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB_campaign "Kyūshū campaign") and [Sword hunt](/wiki/Sword_hunt "Sword hunt")

In 1586 Hideyoshi conquered [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB "Kyūshū"), wresting control from the [Shimazu clan](/wiki/Shimazu_clan "Shimazu clan").[[45]](#cite_note-48) [Toyotomi Hidenaga](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidenaga "Toyotomi Hidenaga"), Hideyoshi's half-brother, landed in the south of [Bungo Province](/wiki/Bungo_Province "Bungo Province") on Kyūshū's eastern coast. Meanwhile, Hideyoshi took his own forces down a more western route, in [Chikuzen Province](/wiki/Chikuzen_Province "Chikuzen Province").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later that year, with a total of 200,000 soldiers against the 30,000 men of the Shimazu forces, the two brothers met in [Satsuma Province](/wiki/Satsuma_Province "Satsuma Province"). They [besieged Kagoshima castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Kagoshima "Siege of Kagoshima"), the Shimazu clan's home. The Shimazu surrendered.[[46]](#cite_note-TurnbullKagoshima-49)

In 1588, Hideyoshi forbade ordinary peasants from owning weapons and launched a [sword hunt](/wiki/Sword_hunt "Sword hunt") to confiscate all such weapons.[[47]](#cite_note-50) The weapons were melted down into building material for the Hall of the Great Buddha at the [Hōkō temple](/wiki/H%C5%8Dk%C5%8D-ji_(Kyoto) "Hōkō-ji (Kyoto)") in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"). which was built by Hideyoshi.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-51) This measure effectively stopped peasant revolts, and ensured greater stability at the expense of freedom of the individual daimyō.[[49]](#cite_note-SwordHunt-52)[[50]](#cite_note-53)

### Odawara Campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Odawara Campaign")]

Main article: [Siege of Odawara (1590)](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)")

In 1590, Hideyoshi carried out the [Odawara Campaign](/wiki/Siege_of_Odawara_(1590) "Siege of Odawara (1590)") against the [Later Hōjō clan](/wiki/Later_H%C5%8Dj%C5%8D_clan "Later Hōjō clan") in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"),[[51]](#cite_note-54) in what historian [Stephen Turnbull](/wiki/Stephen_Turnbull_(historian) "Stephen Turnbull (historian)") refers to as "the most unconventional siege lines in samurai history". The samurai were entertained by everything from concubines, prostitutes, and musicians to [acrobats](/wiki/Acrobat "Acrobat"), fire-eaters, and [jugglers](/wiki/Juggler "Juggler"). The defenders slept on the ramparts with their [teppō](/wiki/Tepp%C5%8D "Teppō") and armor; despite their smaller numbers, they discouraged Hideyoshi from attacking.[[15]](#cite_note-Turnbull-15) Hideyoshi had [Ishigakiyama Ichiya Castle](/wiki/Ishigakiyama_Ichiya_Castle "Ishigakiyama Ichiya Castle") secretly constructed in a nearby forest.[[52]](#cite_note-Odawara-55)[[53]](#cite_note-Kojo-56)[[54]](#cite_note-KO-57)
During the siege, Hideyoshi offered Ieyasu the eight Hōjō-ruled provinces in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"), in exchange for the submission of Ieyasu's five provinces, which Ieyasu accepted.[[55]](#cite_note-Ando_Yuichiro;_Tokugawa_Kanto-58)

In late September of the same year, an uprising broke out in the Yokote Basin, Senboku district ([Senboku District, Akita](/wiki/Senboku_District,_Akita "Senboku District, Akita") in post-Meiji era), in opposition to the Taiko land survey conducted by the Toyotomi government. Although the Senboku rebellion was suppressed later, the result was a disaster for the [Onodera clan](/w/index.php?title=Onodera_clan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Onodera clan (page does not exist)").[[56]](#cite_note-59)

### 1591 activities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 1591 activities")]

Main article: [Sen no Rikyū](/wiki/Sen_no_Riky%C5%AB "Sen no Rikyū")

In February 1591, Hideyoshi ordered [Sen no Rikyū](/wiki/Sen_no_Riky%C5%AB "Sen no Rikyū") to commit suicide, likely in one of his angry outbursts.[[57]](#cite_note-60) Following Rikyū's death, Hideyoshi turned his attention from tea ceremony to [Noh](/wiki/Noh "Noh"), which he had been studying since becoming Imperial Regent. During his brief stay in [Nagoya Castle](/wiki/Nagoya_Castle_(Hizen_Province) "Nagoya Castle (Hizen Province)") in what is today [Saga Prefecture](/wiki/Saga_Prefecture "Saga Prefecture"), on [Kyūshū](/wiki/Ky%C5%ABsh%C5%AB "Kyūshū"), Hideyoshi memorised the *shite* (lead role) parts of ten Noh plays, which he then performed, forcing various *daimyō* to accompany him onstage as the *waki* (secondary, accompanying role). He even performed before the emperor.[[58]](#cite_note-61)

The [Kunohe rebellion](/wiki/Kunohe_rebellion "Kunohe rebellion"), an [insurrection](/wiki/Insurrection "Insurrection") that occurred in [Mutsu Province](/wiki/Mutsu_Province "Mutsu Province") from March 13 to September 4, 1591, began when [Kunohe Masazane](/w/index.php?title=Kunohe_Masazane&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kunohe Masazane (page does not exist)"), a claimant to daimyo of the [Nanbu clan](/wiki/Nanbu_clan "Nanbu clan"), launched a rebellion against his rival [Nanbu Nobunao](/wiki/Nanbu_Nobunao "Nanbu Nobunao") which spread across Mutsu Province. Nobunao was backed by Hideyoshi, who along with sent a large army into the [Tōhoku region](/wiki/T%C5%8Dhoku_region "Tōhoku region") in mid-1591 which quickly defeated the rebels. Hideyoshi's army arrived at [Kunohe Castle](/wiki/Kunohe_Castle "Kunohe Castle") in early September. Masazane, outnumbered, surrendered Kunohe Castle and was executed with the castle defenders. The Kunohe rebellion was the final battle in Hideyoshi's campaigns during the Sengoku period and completed the unification of Japan.[[59]](#cite_note-62)

## Taikō (1592–1598)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Taikō (1592–1598)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Hokoji%28rushanabutsu%29.jpg/250px-Hokoji%28rushanabutsu%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hokoji(rushanabutsu).jpg)

Replica of [Great Buddha of Kyoto](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E4%BA%AC%E3%81%AE%E5%A4%A7%E4%BB%8F "jp:京の大仏"). The Great Buddha of Kyoto was built by Hideyoshi to show off his power.

The future stability of the [Toyotomi dynasty](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") after Hideyoshi's eventual death was put in doubt when his only son, three-year-old Tsurumatsu, died in September 1591, which followed his half-brother [Hidenaga](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidenaga "Toyotomi Hidenaga")'s death from illness earlier that year in February. Hideyoshi subsequently named his nephew [Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu") his heir, adopting him in January 1592. Hideyoshi resigned as *kampaku* to take the title of *taikō* (retired regent), and Hidetsugu succeeded him as *kampaku*.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Hideyoshi_Toyotomi%27s_armor.jpg/250px-Hideyoshi_Toyotomi%27s_armor.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hideyoshi_Toyotomi%27s_armor.jpg)

Replica of Toyotomi Hideyoshi's armor

Hideyoshi adopted [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga")'s dream of a Japanese conquest of [China](/wiki/China "China"), and launched the conquest of the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") [by way of Korea](/wiki/Japanese_invasions_of_Korea_(1592%E2%80%931598) "Japanese invasions of Korea (1592–1598)") (at the time known as Koryu or [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon")).[[60]](#cite_note-63)

In 1592, Hideyoshi began an invasion of Korea with the intent of conquering Korea and eventually Ming China.[[61]](#cite_note-:Ma&Kang-64): 99 In 1593, he attempted but failed at compelling Taiwan into a tributary relationship.[[62]](#cite_note-65): 60 Hideyoshi's explicit war goal was for Japan to replace China at the top of the international order.[[61]](#cite_note-:Ma&Kang-64): 92 Hideyoshi wrote to his adopted son Hidetsugu that "it is not Ming China alone that is destined to be subjugated by us, but India, the Philippines, and many islands in the South Sea will share a like fate."[[61]](#cite_note-:Ma&Kang-64): 99–100

### First campaign against Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: First campaign against Korea")]

Main article: [Imjin War](/wiki/Imjin_War "Imjin War")

In the first campaign, Hideyoshi appointed [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie") as field marshal and sent him to the Korean Peninsula in April 1592. [Konishi Yukinaga](/wiki/Konishi_Yukinaga "Konishi Yukinaga") occupied [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), which was the capital of [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon"), on June 19. After Seoul fell, Japanese commanders held a war council there in June and determined targets of subjugation called *Hachidokuniwari*, literally [Eight(八) Route(道), Country(国) Division(割)](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%96%87%E7%A6%84%E3%83%BB%E6%85%B6%E9%95%B7%E3%81%AE%E5%BD%B9 "ja:文禄・慶長の役"). Each targeted province was attacked by one of the army's eight divisions:[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

* [Pyeongan Province](/wiki/Pyeongan_Province "Pyeongan Province") by the First Division led by [Konishi Yukinaga](/wiki/Konishi_Yukinaga "Konishi Yukinaga").
* [Hamgyong](/wiki/Hamgyong_Province "Hamgyong Province") by the Second Division led by [Katō Kiyomasa](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Kiyomasa "Katō Kiyomasa").
* [Hwanghae Province](/wiki/Hwanghae_Province "Hwanghae Province") by the Third Division led by [Kuroda Nagamasa](/wiki/Kuroda_Nagamasa "Kuroda Nagamasa").
* [Gangwon Province](/wiki/Kangwon_Province_(pre-1910) "Kangwon Province (pre-1910)") by the Fourth Division led by [Mōri Katsunaga](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Katsunaga "Mōri Katsunaga").
* [Chungcheong Province](/wiki/Chungcheong_Province "Chungcheong Province") by the Fifth Division led by [Fukushima Masanori](/wiki/Fukushima_Masanori "Fukushima Masanori").
* [Jeolla Province](/wiki/Jeolla_Province "Jeolla Province") by the Sixth Division led by [Kobayakawa Takakage](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Takakage "Kobayakawa Takakage").
* [Gyeongsang Province](/wiki/Gyeongsang_Province "Gyeongsang Province") by the Seventh Division led by [Mōri Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto").
* [Gyeonggi Province](/wiki/Gyeonggi_Province "Gyeonggi Province") by the Eighth Division led by [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie").

Within four months, Hideyoshi's forces had a route into [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria") and had occupied much of Korea. The Korean king [Seonjo of Joseon](/wiki/Seonjo_of_Joseon "Seonjo of Joseon") escaped to [Uiju](/wiki/Uiju "Uiju") and requested military intervention from China. In 1593, the [Wanli Emperor](/wiki/Wanli_Emperor "Wanli Emperor") of [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") sent an army under general [Li Rusong](/wiki/Li_Rusong "Li Rusong") to block the planned Japanese invasion of China and recapture the Korean peninsula. On January 7, 1593, the Ming relief forces recaptured Pyongyang and surrounded [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul"), but [Kobayakawa Takakage](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Takakage "Kobayakawa Takakage"), [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie"), [Tachibana Muneshige](/wiki/Tachibana_Muneshige "Tachibana Muneshige") and [Kikkawa Hiroie](/wiki/Kikkawa_Hiroie "Kikkawa Hiroie") were able to win the [Battle of Byeokjegwan](/wiki/Battle_of_Byeokjegwan "Battle of Byeokjegwan") north of Seoul (now [Goyang](/wiki/Goyang "Goyang")). At the end of the first campaign, Japan's entire navy was destroyed by Admiral [Yi Sun-sin](/wiki/Yi_Sun-sin "Yi Sun-sin") of Korea, whose base was located in a part of Korea the Japanese could not control. This destroyed Japan's ability to resupply their troops in Seoul, effectively ending the invasion.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Succession dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Succession dispute")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Hideyori_Toyotomi.jpg/250px-Hideyori_Toyotomi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hideyori_Toyotomi.jpg)

Toyotomi Hideyori

Following Hideyoshi's appointment of his nephew [Hidetsugu](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hidetsugu "Toyotomi Hidetsugu") to the position of *[kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku"),* tensions started to develop due to the dual power structure between Hidetsugu, who led the court system, and Hideyoshi, who retained actual military power as retired regent. Although Hideyoshi orchestrated Hidetsugu's rise, the regent's position was governed by established court frameworks, limiting Hideyoshi's ability to bypass precedents. This led to the formation of two factions: the "*Taiko* (Hideyoshi's) group" and the "*Kampaku* (Hidetsugu's) group," which clashed over political and military issues.[[48]](#cite_note-:0-51)

The birth of Hideyoshi's second son in 1593, [Hideyori](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyori "Toyotomi Hideyori"), exacerbated these tensions, as it introduced another potential heir to the Toyotomi dynasty. In July 1595, amidst suspicions of treason and the ongoing Korean invasion, Hidetsugu was stripped of his titles, exiled to [Mount Kōya](/wiki/Mount_K%C5%8Dya "Mount Kōya"), and then ordered to commit suicide in August 1595. Hidetsugu's family members who did not follow his example, including 31 women and several children, were then beheaded in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto").[[48]](#cite_note-:0-51)[[63]](#cite_note-66)[[64]](#cite_note-jk270323-67)[[65]](#cite_note-toyo220516-68)[[66]](#cite_note-yh100324-69) In the aftermath, Hideyoshi solidified his authority by securing loyalty oaths, signed in blood, from magistrates and daimyos including influential damiyo like [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), [Maeda Toshiie](/wiki/Maeda_Toshiie "Maeda Toshiie"), [Ukita Hideie](/wiki/Ukita_Hideie "Ukita Hideie"), [Mori Terumoto](/wiki/M%C5%8Dri_Terumoto "Mōri Terumoto"), and [Kobayakawa Takakage](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Takakage "Kobayakawa Takakage").[[48]](#cite_note-:0-51)

### Second campaign against Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Second campaign against Korea")]

Main article: [Imjin War](/wiki/Imjin_War "Imjin War")

After several years of negotiations, which were broken off after envoys from both sides falsely reported that the opposition had surrendered, Hideyoshi appointed [Kobayakawa Hideaki](/wiki/Kobayakawa_Hideaki "Kobayakawa Hideaki") to lead a renewed invasion of Korea. This invasion met with less success than the first; Japanese troops remained pinned down in [Gyeongsang Province](/wiki/Gyeongsang_Province "Gyeongsang Province"), and although the Japanese forces turned back several Chinese offensives in [Suncheon](/wiki/Suncheon "Suncheon") and [Sacheon](/wiki/Sacheon "Sacheon") in June 1598, they were unable to make further progress as the [Ming](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty") army prepared for a final assault. While Hideyoshi's [battle at Sacheon](/wiki/Battle_of_Sacheon_(1598) "Battle of Sacheon (1598)"), led by [Shimazu Yoshihiro](/wiki/Shimazu_Yoshihiro "Shimazu Yoshihiro"), was a major Japanese victory, all three parties to the war were exhausted. He told his commander in Korea, "Don't let my soldiers become spirits in a foreign land.".[[4]](#cite_note-Holmes-4)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/Houkokubyo.jpg/250px-Houkokubyo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houkokubyo.jpg)

Houkokubyo (Mausoleum of Toyotomi Hideyoshi) [Higashiyama-ku](/wiki/Higashiyama-ku,_Kyoto "Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto"), [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto")

Toyotomi Hideyoshi died at [Fushimi Castle](/wiki/Fushimi_Castle "Fushimi Castle") on September 18, 1598 ([Keichō](/wiki/Keich%C5%8D "Keichō") 3, 18th day of the 8th month). His last words, delivered to his closest *daimyō* and generals, were "I depend upon you for everything. I have no other thoughts to leave behind. It is sad to part from you." His death was kept secret by the [Council of Five Elders](/wiki/Council_of_Five_Elders "Council of Five Elders") to preserve morale, and they ordered Japanese forces in [Korea](/wiki/Korea "Korea") to return to Japan.

According to the *Tokugawa Jikki* record, Hideyoshi held a secret meeting with [Koide Hidemasa](/wiki/Koide_Hidemasa "Koide Hidemasa") and [Katagiri Katsumoto](/wiki/Katagiri_Katsumoto "Katagiri Katsumoto") where he shared his regret of launching invasions of Korea. Hideyoshi also instructed Hidemasa and Katsumoto to guide Hideyori into making an alliance with Ieyasu, as he predicted the power of the [Tokugawa clan](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") would grow unchecked after his death, and the only solution for the Toyotomi clan to survive was to not oppose Ieyasu.[[67]](#cite_note-70)

After Hideyoshi's death, the other members of the Council of Five Elders were unable to keep Ieyasu's ambitions in check. Two of Hideyoshi's [top generals](/wiki/Seven_Spears_of_Shizugatake "Seven Spears of Shizugatake"), [Katō Kiyomasa](/wiki/Kat%C5%8D_Kiyomasa "Katō Kiyomasa") and [Fukushima Masanori](/wiki/Fukushima_Masanori "Fukushima Masanori"), had fought bravely during the war but returned to find the [Toyotomi clan](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan") [castellan](/wiki/Castellan "Castellan") [Ishida Mitsunari](/wiki/Ishida_Mitsunari "Ishida Mitsunari") in power. He held the generals in contempt, and they sided with Ieyasu. Hideyori lost the power his father once held, and Ieyasu's power was consolidated when his Eastern Army defeated the Mitsunari's Western Army at the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara") in 1600. Ieyasu, who was appointed as a [shogun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun") in 1603 and established the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate"), attacked [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle") twice in 1614 and 1615 (the [Siege of Osaka](/wiki/Siege_of_Osaka "Siege of Osaka")), forcing Hideyoshi's concubine [Yodo-dono](/wiki/Yodo-dono "Yodo-dono") and Hideyori to commit suicide, destroying the [Toyotomi clan](/wiki/Toyotomi_clan "Toyotomi clan").[[68]](#cite_note-sekigahara-71)[[69]](#cite_note-osakajk-72)

It is now believed that Hideyoshi's loss of all his adult heirs, leaving only the five-year-old Hideyori as his successor, was the primary reason for the weakening of the Toyotomi regime and its eventual downfall.[[64]](#cite_note-jk270323-67)[[65]](#cite_note-toyo220516-68)[[66]](#cite_note-yh100324-69)

## Character and policy analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Character and policy analysis")]

Described as a "hitotarashi" (a person with natural charisma or a master charmer), Hideyoshi was skilled at winning people over and mastering human psychology. This resulted in favorable views from both his superiors and his subordinates, and for some opposing warlords to open up to him and agree to surrender after meeting him in person.[[70]](#cite_note-73) According to Japanese historian [Watanabe Daimon](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E6%B8%A1%E9%82%8A%E5%A4%A7%E9%96%80 "ja:渡邊大門"), Hideyoshi had a deep inferiority complex which influenced his behavior after he became regent, as he often toyed with or pranked his vassals. This behavior may have stemmed from his humble origin and experiences during Nobunaga's lifetime, such as not being permitted to ride alongside other generals who hailed from samurai class and being required to dismount before bowing.[[71]](#cite_note-74)

### Religious policy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Religious policy")]

In 1587, while trying to establish control in some parts of Kyushu, Hideyoshi encountered Buddhist temples that had been sacked by Catholic forces attempting to forcibly convert the island.[[72]](#cite_note-75) In response, he issued the [Bateren Edict](/wiki/Bateren_Edict "Bateren Edict")[[d]](#cite_note-76) on June 19, 1587, which ordered the expulsion of [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") from Japan. Promulgated during Hideyoshi's campaign to unify Kyushu, the edict was a response to several perceived threats posed by Christianity,[[73]](#cite_note-77) to exert greater control over the *[Kirishitan](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan")* *daimyō*[[74]](#cite_note-78) or to prohibit human trafficking.[[75]](#cite_note-Japanslavesportuguese;_Watanabe_Daimon-79) Around that time, at least 50,000 Japanese people were sold overseas as slaves, mainly by Portuguese merchants.[[76]](#cite_note-80) This stance was further shown in Hideyoshi's letter sent in 25 July 1590 to [Alessandro Valignano](/wiki/Alessandro_Valignano "Alessandro Valignano"), which content are similar the contents of a letter he sent to the ruler of [Joseon](/wiki/Joseon "Joseon"). In those letters, Hideyoshi expressed his unique religious view that Indian Buddhism, Chinese Confucianism, and Japanese Shinto are fundamentally one unit, while also warned that he would no longer tolerate the propagation of Christianity ("evil religion" in Hideyoshi's letter), and he would no longer allow Christian missionaries to enter the country, albeit he still allowed merchants from Europe (Nanban) to enter and trade.[[77]](#cite_note-81)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Painting_of_the_Nagasaki_Martyrs.jpg/250px-Painting_of_the_Nagasaki_Martyrs.jpg)](/wiki/File:Painting_of_the_Nagasaki_Martyrs.jpg)

[The 26 Christian martyrs of Nagasaki](/wiki/26_Martyrs_of_Japan "26 Martyrs of Japan"), 18–19th century, Choir of La Recoleta, [Cuzco](/wiki/Cuzco "Cuzco")

In January 1597, Toyotomi Hideyoshi had twenty-six Christians arrested as an example to Japanese who wanted to convert to [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"). They are known as the [Twenty-six Martyrs of Japan](/wiki/Twenty-six_Martyrs_of_Japan "Twenty-six Martyrs of Japan"). They included five European [Franciscan](/wiki/Franciscans "Franciscans") [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary"), one [Mexican Franciscan](/wiki/Philip_of_Jesus "Philip of Jesus") missionary, three Japanese [Jesuits](/wiki/Jesuits "Jesuits") and seventeen Japanese [laymen](/wiki/Laymen "Laymen") including three young boys. They were tortured, mutilated, and paraded through towns across Japan. On February 5, they were executed in [Nagasaki](/wiki/Nagasaki "Nagasaki") by public [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion").[[78]](#cite_note-82)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Osaka_Castle_02bs3200.jpg/500px-Osaka_Castle_02bs3200.jpg)](/wiki/File:Osaka_Castle_02bs3200.jpg)

A replicated [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle") has been created on the site of Hideyoshi's great *[donjon](/wiki/Donjon "Donjon").* The iconic castle has become a symbol of [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka")'s re-emergence as a great city after its devastation in [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

By 18 August 1915 Hideyoshi was given posthumous rank of [Senior First Rank](/wiki/List_of_Japanese_court_ranks,_positions_and_hereditary_titles "List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Toyotomi Hideyoshi changed [Japanese society](/wiki/Japanese_society "Japanese society") in many ways. These include the imposition of a rigid [class](/wiki/Social_class "Social class") structure, restrictions on travel, and surveys of land and production.[[79]](#cite_note-83)

[Class reforms](/wiki/Separation_Edict "Separation Edict") affected commoners and warriors. During the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), it had become common for peasants to become warriors, or for samurai to farm due to the constant uncertainty caused by the lack of centralised government and always tentative peace. Upon taking control, Hideyoshi decreed that all peasants be disarmed completely.[[80]](#cite_note-84) Conversely, he required samurai to leave the land and take up residence in the castle towns.[[81]](#cite_note-85)[[82]](#cite_note-86)

Furthermore, he ordered comprehensive surveys and a [complete census](/wiki/Population_Census_Edict "Population Census Edict") of Japan. Once this was done and all citizens were registered, he required all Japanese to stay in their respective *[han](/wiki/Han_(administrative_division) "Han (administrative division)")* (fiefs) unless they obtained official permission to go elsewhere. This ensured order in a period when bandits still roamed the countryside and peace was still new. The land surveys formed the basis for systematic taxation.[[83]](#cite_note-87)

In 1590, Hideyoshi completed construction of the [Osaka Castle](/wiki/Osaka_Castle "Osaka Castle"), the largest and most formidable in all Japan, to guard the western approaches to [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"). In that same year, Hideyoshi banned "unfree labour" or [slavery in Japan](/wiki/Slavery_in_Japan "Slavery in Japan"),[[84]](#cite_note-88) but forms of contract and indentured labour persisted alongside the period penal codes' [forced labour](/wiki/Forced_labour "Forced labour").[[85]](#cite_note-89)

Hideyoshi also influenced the material [culture of Japan](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan"). He lavished time and money on the [Japanese tea ceremony](/wiki/Japanese_tea_ceremony "Japanese tea ceremony"), collecting implements, sponsoring lavish social events, and patronizing acclaimed masters. As interest in the tea ceremony rose among the ruling class, so too did the demand for fine ceramic implements, and during the course of the Korean campaigns, not only were large quantities of prized [ceramic](/wiki/Ceramic "Ceramic") ware confiscated but many Korean artisans were forcibly relocated to Japan.[[86]](#cite_note-90)

Inspired by the dazzling [Golden Pavilion](/wiki/Kinkaku-ji "Kinkaku-ji") in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), he had the [Golden Tea Room](/wiki/Golden_Tea_Room "Golden Tea Room") constructed, which was covered with [gold leaf](/wiki/Gold_leaf "Gold leaf") and lined inside with red gossamer. Using this mobile innovation, he was able to practice the tea ceremony wherever he went, displaying his power and status at all times.[[87]](#cite_note-91)

Politically, he set up a governmental system that balanced out the most powerful Japanese warlords (or *daimyō*). A council was created to include the most influential lords. At the same time, a regent was designated to be in command.[[88]](#cite_note-92)

Just before his death, Hideyoshi hoped to set up a system stable enough to survive until his son grew old enough to become the next leader.[[89]](#cite_note-93)

Ieyasu left in place the majority of Hideyoshi's decrees and built his shogunate upon them. This ensured that Hideyoshi's cultural legacy remained. In a letter to his wife, Hideyoshi wrote:

> I mean to do glorious deeds and I am ready for a long siege, with provisions and gold and silver in plenty, so as to return in triumph and leave a great name behind me. I desire you to understand this and to tell it to everybody.[[90]](#cite_note-94)

## Names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Names")]

The [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") sources of the time referred to him as *Cuambacondono*[[91]](#cite_note-Taicosama-95) (from *kampaku* and the honorific *[-dono](/wiki/-dono "-dono")*) and "emperor *Taicosama*"[[91]](#cite_note-Taicosama-95) (from *taikō*, a retired *kampaku* (see [Sesshō and Kampaku](/wiki/Sessh%C5%8D_and_Kampaku "Sesshō and Kampaku")), and the honorific *[-sama](/wiki/Japanese_titles "Japanese titles")*).

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

### Films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Films")]

In the 1949 Mexican hagiographic film *[Philip of Jesus](/wiki/Philip_of_Jesus "Philip of Jesus")*, [Luis Aceves Castañeda](/wiki/Luis_Aceves_Casta%C3%B1eda "Luis Aceves Castañeda") plays a character corresponding to Hideyoshi but named "Emperor Iroyoshi Taikosama".[[92]](#cite_note-96)

In the 2009 Japanese [historical fantasy](/wiki/Historical_fantasy "Historical fantasy") film *[Goemon](/wiki/Goemon_(film) "Goemon (film)")*, Toyotomi Hideyoshi (played by [Eiji Okuda](/wiki/Eiji_Okuda "Eiji Okuda")) features as the principal antagonist to the film's protagonist, [Ishikawa Goemon](/wiki/Ishikawa_Goemon "Ishikawa Goemon"). This is based on the tradition that Goemon was executed for his failed attempt to assassinate Toyotomi Hideyoshi in 1594,[[93]](#cite_note-97) but the film otherwise bears little resemblance to either historical events or the received tradition. In the film, Goemon murders Hideyoshi's stand-in, avoids his execution by boiling (being replaced by an associate), succeeds in murdering Hideyoshi on a later occasion, and survives to intervene in the [Battle of Sekigahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Sekigahara "Battle of Sekigahara"). Goemon is portrayed as the faithful retainer and avenger of [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"), unhistorically depicted as the victim of Toyotomi Hideyoshi. All of this is counter to historical facts; tradition credits Goemon with serving Nobunaga's enemies the [Miyoshi clan](/wiki/Miyoshi_clan "Miyoshi clan") and his murderer, [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide"), as well as with failed murder attempts on both Oda Nobunaga and Toyotomi Hideyoshi.[[94]](#cite_note-98)

Hideyoshi is portrayed by actor/director [Takeshi Kitano](/wiki/Takeshi_Kitano "Takeshi Kitano") in his 2023 film *[Kubi](/wiki/Kubi_(film) "Kubi (film)")*.

### Anime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Anime")]

In the anime series *[Great Pretender](/wiki/Great_Pretender_(TV_series) "Great Pretender (TV series)")* (2020), Hideyoshi is referenced many times by Laurent Thierry, one of the central protagonists of the series.[[95]](#cite_note-99)

### Documentary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Documentary")]

In the [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") documentary series *[Age of Samurai: Battle for Japan](/wiki/Age_of_Samurai:_Battle_for_Japan "Age of Samurai: Battle for Japan")* (2021), Hideyoshi is portrayed by Masami Kosaka. The show depicts his life and rise to power.[[96]](#cite_note-100)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Toyotomi_Hideyoshi&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Television")]

Actor [Naoto Takenaka](/wiki/Naoto_Takenaka "Naoto Takenaka") portrays Toyotomi Hideyoshi in the 1996 NHK drama *[Hideyoshi](/wiki/Hideyoshi_(TV_series) "Hideyoshi (TV series)")*, which shows his life from his time under Oda Nobunaga to his rise as a leader himself who helped to unify Japan. It earned an average TV rating of 30.5% running from January 7 – December 22, 1996. He would reprise this role again in [Nioh 2](/wiki/Nioh_2 "Nioh 2"). Additionally, actor [Yukijirō Hotaru](/wiki/Yukijir%C5%8D_Hotaru "Yukijirō Hotaru") plays The Taikō (Nakamura Hidetoshi), a character based on Toyotomi Hideyoshi, in the 2024 miniseries *[Shōgun](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dgun_(2024_miniseries) "Shōgun (2024 miniseries)")*.[[97]](#cite_note-101)
